questions.prompt: Scarcity is best described as the fundamental economic problem because it arises whenever
choices: a government chooses to ration goods during a war.
wants exceed the resources available to satisfy them.
consumers boycott a product because of high prices.
firms cannot find workers willing to accept the offered wage.
questions.correctText: wants exceed the resources available to satisfy them.
questions.explanation: Scarcity is the universal condition that human wants outstrip finite resources, which is why every society must answer what, how, and for whom to produce.

questions.prompt: A student has three hours and can spend it studying for a math quiz, working a shift that pays 15 dollars per hour, or sleeping. If she chooses to study, the opportunity cost of studying is
choices: the dollar value of the wages plus the value of the sleep she gives up.
only the wages she would have earned, because sleep has no monetary value.
the value of her next best forgone alternative.
the sum of all activities she did not choose.
questions.correctText: the value of her next best forgone alternative.
questions.explanation: Opportunity cost is defined as the value of the single next-best alternative forgone, not the sum of all alternatives sacrificed.

questions.prompt: A production possibilities curve (PPC) bowed outward from the origin reflects
choices: constant opportunity costs across all combinations.
increasing opportunity costs as production of one good expands.
decreasing opportunity costs due to specialization.
an economy operating below full employment.
questions.correctText: increasing opportunity costs as production of one good expands.
questions.explanation: A bowed-out PPC reflects that resources are not equally productive in all uses, so shifting more resources toward one good means giving up larger and larger quantities of the other.

questions.prompt: Country X can produce either 40 tons of wheat or 20 tons of steel per year. Country Y can produce either 30 tons of wheat or 30 tons of steel per year. Based on comparative advantage, which country should specialize in steel?
choices: Country X, because it has the larger wheat output.
Country Y, because it gives up only 1 ton of wheat per ton of steel while X gives up 2.
Neither country, because both should produce both goods equally.
Country X, because it has a higher absolute output in wheat.
questions.correctText: Country Y, because it gives up only 1 ton of wheat per ton of steel while X gives up 2.
questions.explanation: Comparative advantage goes to the lower-opportunity-cost producer. Country Y gives up 1 wheat per steel; Country X gives up 2 wheat per steel, so Y should make steel.

questions.prompt: A rational consumer should continue purchasing additional units of a good as long as
choices: total utility is positive.
marginal utility per dollar exceeds zero.
marginal benefit exceeds marginal cost.
average benefit exceeds average cost.
questions.correctText: marginal benefit exceeds marginal cost.
questions.explanation: Marginal analysis tells us to keep consuming until MB equals MC. Stopping earlier leaves gains unrealized; going further imposes losses.

questions.prompt: In the market for butter, which of the following would shift the demand curve to the right?
choices: A decrease in the price of butter.
A successful health campaign promoting butter as nutritious.
An increase in the price of corn used as cattle feed.
A new technology that lowers production costs for butter.
questions.correctText: A successful health campaign promoting butter as nutritious.
questions.explanation: Tastes and preferences are a demand shifter. A price change moves along the curve; the feed and technology changes are supply shifters.

questions.prompt: If the price of leather (an input) rises sharply, the market for leather handbags will experience
choices: a leftward shift of the supply curve, raising equilibrium price and lowering equilibrium quantity.
a rightward shift of the supply curve, lowering equilibrium price.
a rightward shift of the demand curve.
no change, because consumers absorb all input cost changes.
questions.correctText: a leftward shift of the supply curve, raising equilibrium price and lowering equilibrium quantity.
questions.explanation: Higher input prices reduce supply at every price. The supply curve shifts left, equilibrium price rises, and equilibrium quantity falls.

questions.prompt: A government imposes a binding price ceiling on rental apartments set below the equilibrium rent. The most likely short-run result is
choices: a surplus of available apartments.
a shortage of apartments and reduced incentive for landlords to maintain quality.
an immediate increase in apartment construction.
an equilibrium with no welfare loss.
questions.correctText: a shortage of apartments and reduced incentive for landlords to maintain quality.
questions.explanation: A binding price ceiling holds rent below equilibrium, so quantity demanded exceeds quantity supplied and landlords have less revenue to invest in upkeep.

questions.prompt: When the price of a movie ticket rises from 10 dollars to 12 dollars, quantity demanded falls from 220 to 180 tickets. Using the midpoint formula, the price elasticity of demand is approximately
choices: 0.5, indicating inelastic demand.
1.0, indicating unit-elastic demand.
1.2, indicating elastic demand.
2.5, indicating perfectly elastic demand.
questions.correctText: 1.2, indicating elastic demand.
questions.explanation: Midpoint % change in Q is 40/200 = 20%; midpoint % change in P is 2/11 ≈ 18.2%. PED ≈ 20/18.2 ≈ 1.1 (rounded to about 1.2), which is elastic.

questions.prompt: If the cross-price elasticity of demand between Good A and Good B is calculated to be negative 1.4, the two goods are most likely
choices: unrelated goods.
complements, such as printers and ink cartridges.
substitutes, such as Coke and Pepsi.
inferior goods.
questions.correctText: complements, such as printers and ink cartridges.
questions.explanation: Negative cross-price elasticity means a rise in one good's price reduces quantity demanded of the other, which is the defining pattern for complements.

questions.prompt: Income rises by 10 percent and a household's quantity demanded of bus tickets falls by 6 percent. The income elasticity of demand is
choices: +0.6, identifying bus tickets as a normal good.
-0.6, identifying bus tickets as an inferior good.
+1.6, identifying bus tickets as a luxury.
0, identifying bus tickets as a Giffen good.
questions.correctText: -0.6, identifying bus tickets as an inferior good.
questions.explanation: Income elasticity = % change in Qd / % change in income = -6/10 = -0.6. A negative income elasticity defines an inferior good.

questions.prompt: A per-unit excise tax of 2 dollars is placed on producers in a competitive market. In the new equilibrium, total tax revenue collected equals
choices: the area of the deadweight-loss triangle.
2 dollars multiplied by the post-tax quantity sold.
the original equilibrium price multiplied by quantity.
the producer surplus before the tax was imposed.
questions.correctText: 2 dollars multiplied by the post-tax quantity sold.
questions.explanation: Tax revenue equals the per-unit tax times the new lower equilibrium quantity that consumers and producers transact at after the tax.

questions.prompt: Deadweight loss from a per-unit tax on a competitive market is largest when
choices: both supply and demand are highly inelastic.
both supply and demand are highly elastic.
the government imposes no tax at all.
the market is already in long-run equilibrium without the tax.
questions.correctText: both supply and demand are highly elastic.
questions.explanation: Elastic curves mean consumers and producers respond strongly to price changes, so a tax shrinks quantity by a lot, enlarging the deadweight-loss triangle.

questions.prompt: Marginal product begins to decline before average product does because
choices: fixed costs always rise faster than variable costs.
of diminishing marginal returns, where each added unit of the variable input adds less output than the previous unit.
average product is unrelated to marginal product.
firms experience economies of scale in the short run.
questions.correctText: of diminishing marginal returns, where each added unit of the variable input adds less output than the previous unit.
questions.explanation: With fixed capital, adding more workers eventually means each new worker contributes less extra output. Average product keeps rising until marginal product cuts through it from above.

questions.prompt: A firm pays 8,000 dollars per month in rent regardless of output. This rent is classified as
choices: a variable cost because it is paid in dollars.
a fixed cost because it does not vary with the quantity produced in the short run.
a marginal cost because each unit shares it.
an opportunity cost only.
questions.correctText: a fixed cost because it does not vary with the quantity produced in the short run.
questions.explanation: Fixed costs are independent of output in the short run. Rent under a contract is a textbook example.

questions.prompt: The marginal cost curve will always intersect the
choices: average fixed cost curve at its highest point.
average variable cost and average total cost curves at their minimum points.
demand curve at the profit-maximizing price.
supply curve at the shutdown point.
questions.correctText: average variable cost and average total cost curves at their minimum points.
questions.explanation: When marginal cost is below an average curve, that average is falling; when above, it is rising. So MC crosses AVC and ATC exactly at their minima.

questions.prompt: A perfectly competitive firm should shut down production in the short run if
choices: price is below average total cost but above average variable cost.
price is below average variable cost at every level of output.
the firm is earning zero economic profit.
marginal cost is rising.
questions.correctText: price is below average variable cost at every level of output.
questions.explanation: If price cannot cover variable costs, producing makes losses larger than the fixed costs already lost. Shut down and lose only fixed costs.

questions.prompt: A perfectly competitive firm produces at the output level where
choices: marginal revenue equals marginal cost and equals price.
average total cost equals marginal revenue.
total revenue is maximized.
fixed cost equals variable cost.
questions.correctText: marginal revenue equals marginal cost and equals price.
questions.explanation: Because a competitive firm is a price taker, P = MR. Profit max requires MR = MC, so P = MR = MC at the optimal output.

questions.prompt: In long-run equilibrium under perfect competition, a typical firm earns
choices: positive economic profit due to fixed costs.
zero economic profit because entry and exit drive price to minimum ATC.
negative economic profit indefinitely.
accounting losses but positive economic profit.
questions.correctText: zero economic profit because entry and exit drive price to minimum ATC.
questions.explanation: Free entry erases profits and exit eliminates losses, so in the long run price settles at the minimum of average total cost and economic profit is zero.

questions.prompt: A perfectly competitive market in long-run equilibrium is described as
choices: allocatively efficient because P = MC, and productively efficient because firms produce at minimum ATC.
allocatively inefficient because price exceeds marginal cost.
productively inefficient because firms operate above minimum ATC.
neither efficient nor competitive.
questions.correctText: allocatively efficient because P = MC, and productively efficient because firms produce at minimum ATC.
questions.explanation: Long-run competitive equilibrium delivers both kinds of efficiency: price equals marginal cost (allocative) and output is at minimum average total cost (productive).

questions.prompt: A competitive firm sells output at 12 dollars per unit. Marginal cost is 8 dollars and rising. To maximize profit, the firm should
choices: decrease output, because marginal cost is below price.
increase output, because marginal revenue still exceeds marginal cost.
keep output unchanged.
shut down immediately.
questions.correctText: increase output, because marginal revenue still exceeds marginal cost.
questions.explanation: P = MR = 12 and MC = 8, so each extra unit adds 4 dollars to profit. Expand output until MC rises to meet 12.

questions.prompt: A single-price monopolist maximizes profit by producing the quantity where
choices: price equals marginal cost.
marginal revenue equals marginal cost, then charging the price the demand curve will bear at that quantity.
average total cost equals price.
demand equals supply.
questions.correctText: marginal revenue equals marginal cost, then charging the price the demand curve will bear at that quantity.
questions.explanation: Monopolies set MR = MC for the profit-maximizing quantity and then read price off the demand curve, which lies above MR.

questions.prompt: Compared with the competitive outcome, a profit-maximizing single-price monopolist generally
choices: produces more output at a lower price.
produces less output at a higher price, generating a deadweight loss.
produces the same output but charges a lower price.
achieves both productive and allocative efficiency.
questions.correctText: produces less output at a higher price, generating a deadweight loss.
questions.explanation: Because the monopolist restricts quantity to where MR = MC, output is smaller and price is higher than in competition, creating deadweight loss because P exceeds MC.

questions.prompt: A theater that charges student and senior discounts while charging full price to other adults is engaging in
choices: predatory pricing.
third-degree price discrimination, separating buyers by elasticity of demand.
perfect competition.
natural monopoly pricing.
questions.correctText: third-degree price discrimination, separating buyers by elasticity of demand.
questions.explanation: Third-degree price discrimination charges different prices to different identifiable groups whose elasticities differ. Students and seniors have more elastic demand and get lower prices.

questions.prompt: A perfectly price-discriminating monopolist (first-degree) compared with a single-price monopolist
choices: produces less output and earns smaller profit.
produces the same output as a competitive market and captures all consumer surplus as profit, eliminating deadweight loss.
always charges a uniform low price.
produces output where price equals average total cost.
questions.correctText: produces the same output as a competitive market and captures all consumer surplus as profit, eliminating deadweight loss.
questions.explanation: A perfect price discriminator charges each buyer their willingness to pay, so it produces until P = MC like a competitive market, but it pockets all surplus and leaves zero deadweight loss.

questions.prompt: A monopolistically competitive firm in long-run equilibrium
choices: earns positive economic profit and produces at minimum ATC.
earns zero economic profit but produces with excess capacity, where ATC is above its minimum.
produces where P = MC and minimum ATC.
behaves identically to a perfectly competitive firm.
questions.correctText: earns zero economic profit but produces with excess capacity, where ATC is above its minimum.
questions.explanation: Entry drives demand for each firm down until the demand curve is tangent to ATC, giving zero profit. But that tangency occurs to the left of minimum ATC, so the firm has unused capacity.

questions.prompt: Two firms in a duopoly each must choose to charge a high price or a low price. If both charge high, each earns 80; if both charge low, each earns 40; if one charges high while the other charges low, the high-price firm earns 10 and the low-price firm earns 100. The dominant strategy for each firm is to
choices: charge a high price, because cooperation always pays.
charge a low price, because low yields a higher payoff regardless of the rival's choice.
alternate prices each period.
leave the market entirely.
questions.correctText: charge a low price, because low yields a higher payoff regardless of the rival's choice.
questions.explanation: If the rival charges high, low earns 100 versus 80. If the rival charges low, low earns 40 versus 10. Low dominates either way, producing the Nash equilibrium at (40, 40).

questions.prompt: Which market structure is characterized by a small number of interdependent firms, significant barriers to entry, and the use of strategic behavior such as collusion or game theory?
choices: Perfect competition
Oligopoly
Monopolistic competition
Pure monopoly
questions.correctText: Oligopoly
questions.explanation: Oligopoly features a few large firms whose decisions depend on each other's reactions, making game theory and strategic interaction central to analyzing pricing and output.

questions.prompt: In a competitive labor market, a firm hires workers up to the point where
choices: the wage equals average product times price.
the wage equals the marginal revenue product of labor.
marginal product is zero.
average revenue equals marginal cost of labor.
questions.correctText: the wage equals the marginal revenue product of labor.
questions.explanation: Profit-maximizing factor demand follows MRP = wage. Hire while the next worker's added revenue exceeds the wage; stop when they are equal.

questions.prompt: Demand for skilled welders is considered a derived demand because it
choices: depends directly on workers' personal preferences for welding.
depends on the demand for the goods that welders help to produce.
is independent of product market conditions.
is determined entirely by government regulation.
questions.correctText: depends on the demand for the goods that welders help to produce.
questions.explanation: Firms hire labor not for its own sake but because labor produces goods consumers want. So the labor demand is derived from product demand.

questions.prompt: An increase in the price of the output that workers produce will, all else equal,
choices: shift the labor demand curve to the right because MRP increases at every quantity.
shift the labor supply curve to the right.
leave wages and employment unchanged.
lower the marginal product of labor.
questions.correctText: shift the labor demand curve to the right because MRP increases at every quantity.
questions.explanation: MRP equals marginal product times price. A higher product price raises MRP at every employment level, shifting labor demand outward and raising equilibrium wage and employment.

questions.prompt: A monopsony in a labor market
choices: hires more workers and pays a higher wage than a competitive labor market.
faces an upward-sloping labor supply curve and hires fewer workers at a lower wage than a competitive market.
faces a perfectly elastic labor supply curve.
has no influence on the wage rate.
questions.correctText: faces an upward-sloping labor supply curve and hires fewer workers at a lower wage than a competitive market.
questions.explanation: A sole buyer of labor faces the entire upward-sloping supply curve, so the marginal cost of labor lies above the wage. Setting MCL = MRP yields fewer workers and a lower wage than competition.

questions.prompt: A profit-maximizing firm uses two inputs, labor and capital. The least-cost combination of inputs occurs where
choices: the marginal product of labor equals the marginal product of capital.
MPL divided by wage equals MPK divided by the rental price of capital.
total product is maximized regardless of input prices.
marginal cost equals zero.
questions.correctText: MPL divided by wage equals MPK divided by the rental price of capital.
questions.explanation: Cost minimization requires equal marginal product per dollar across inputs. If one ratio is higher, the firm could swap dollars into that input and produce more cheaply.

questions.prompt: A widget firm hires labor in a perfectly competitive labor market at a wage of 18 dollars per hour. The fourth worker raises output by 3 widgets per hour, and the widgets sell for 7 dollars each. Should the firm hire the fourth worker?
choices: Yes, because MRP of 21 dollars exceeds the wage of 18 dollars.
No, because MRP of 21 dollars is less than the wage of 18 dollars.
No, because output is decreasing.
Yes, but only if fixed costs are zero.
questions.correctText: Yes, because MRP of 21 dollars exceeds the wage of 18 dollars.
questions.explanation: MRP equals marginal product times price, or 3 times 7 = 21 dollars. Since MRP of 21 is above the wage of 18, hiring the worker adds 3 dollars to profit.

questions.prompt: A factory emits pollution that harms downstream residents who are not compensated. From a market-efficiency standpoint, this generates a
choices: positive externality, so output is below the socially optimal level.
negative externality, so private output exceeds the socially optimal level.
public good, so output is provided efficiently.
natural monopoly, so price exceeds marginal cost.
questions.correctText: negative externality, so private output exceeds the socially optimal level.
questions.explanation: Pollution imposes external costs the firm ignores, so marginal social cost exceeds marginal private cost. The free market overproduces relative to the social optimum.

questions.prompt: A per-unit Pigouvian tax that fully corrects a negative externality is set equal to
choices: the firm's average total cost at the private equilibrium quantity.
the marginal external cost at the socially optimal quantity.
the firm's accounting profit per unit.
the price elasticity of demand.
questions.correctText: the marginal external cost at the socially optimal quantity.
questions.explanation: A Pigouvian tax internalizes the externality by raising marginal private cost to equal marginal social cost. The right tax equals the marginal external damage at the efficient output.

questions.prompt: Public goods are typically under-provided by private markets because they are
choices: rival in consumption and excludable.
non-rival and non-excludable, so consumers free-ride.
always produced by monopolies that restrict output.
subject to network effects that raise prices.
questions.correctText: non-rival and non-excludable, so consumers free-ride.
questions.explanation: Non-rivalry means one person's use does not reduce another's; non-excludability means non-payers cannot be kept out. Together they cause free-riding, so private firms cannot recover costs.

questions.prompt: A Lorenz curve that lies very close to the 45-degree line of perfect equality corresponds to
choices: a Gini coefficient close to 1, indicating high inequality.
a Gini coefficient close to 0, indicating low inequality.
a negative Gini coefficient, indicating subsidized incomes.
an undefined Gini coefficient.
questions.correctText: a Gini coefficient close to 0, indicating low inequality.
questions.explanation: The Gini coefficient is the share of the area between the Lorenz curve and the 45-degree line. A curve hugging the line means little area and a Gini near zero.

questions.prompt: A tax structure in which the average tax rate rises as taxable income rises is described as
choices: regressive.
proportional.
progressive.
lump-sum.
questions.correctText: progressive.
questions.explanation: A progressive system collects a larger percentage from higher incomes. Regressive does the opposite; proportional applies a constant rate.

questions.prompt: Antitrust laws and regulation of natural monopolies are government responses primarily intended to
choices: raise tax revenue from large corporations.
address market failures associated with significant market power that leads to deadweight loss.
force firms to operate where price equals average fixed cost.
eliminate all forms of imperfect competition immediately.
questions.correctText: address market failures associated with significant market power that leads to deadweight loss.
questions.explanation: When firms have substantial market power, output is restricted and price exceeds marginal cost, creating deadweight loss. Antitrust and regulation aim to push outcomes closer to efficient levels.
